info.title: NookOS API
info.description: The open workspace operating system for modern software teams.

get.summary: GET /api/v1/auth/dev-accounts — who you can sign in as, in dev mode.
get.description:
Dev only, and unauthenticated by necessity: it is what the login screen
reads before anybody is signed in. It returns emails and display names of
accounts that already exist — no credentials, no tokens — and it is refused
outright unless `AUTH_DEV_MODE` is on and this is not production, which is
the same gate `dev_login` itself uses.

It exists because testing authorization requires BEING different people, and
a model you cannot switch between users to exercise is a model nobody
exercises.

post.summary:
POST /api/v1/auth/dev-login — dev/CI escape hatch. Compiled in, but
hard-refused unless AUTH_DEV_MODE=true (and never in production).
POST /api/v1/auth/local/bootstrap — claim an unclaimed instance.
post.description:
Open only while there are no users at all. After that it is closed
permanently, so the window is "between `docker compose up` and the first
sign-in" rather than something an operator has to remember to turn off.

post.summary: POST /api/v1/auth/local/login
POST /api/v1/auth/local/password — change your own password.

get.summary: Extends `/auth/providers` with what local sign-in offers right now.

post.summary: POST /api/v1/auth/local/users — an admin creates an account.
POST /api/v1/auth/logout

get.summary: GET /api/v1/auth/me

GET /api/v1/auth/providers — unauthenticated: what sign-in methods exist,
so the login screen never offers a dead button.

post.summary:
`POST /api/v1/auth/verify-email/confirm` — consume a token and mark the
address verified. Token-authenticated (works from any browser). A consumed
or expired token is refused (AC-2); re-confirming an already-verified address
is a no-op success (AC-5).

`POST /api/v1/auth/verify-email/request` — issue a token and email the link.
Best-effort: a mail-transport failure is reported, never a 500 (AC-5).

get.summary:
`GET /api/v1/auth/verify-email/status` — is this user's email verified, and
can they request a local verification email? (AC-4)

post.summary:
Archive every live task in one completed/canceled column at once (AC-4).
Refused for any other column type — bulk archive is finished-work cleanup,
not a way to sweep in-progress work off the board (NG-3).

parameters.name: kind
parameters.description: Kind prefix filter, e.g. "session." matches session.started/exited.

post.summary:
Queue feedback. Picks up the configured workspace unless one is given, and
delivers into the named session — creating it if this is the first time.

get.summary: Where feedback is configured to go, if anywhere yet.

put.summary: Point feedback at a repo and branch.
put.description:
Separate from submitting because the target has to be changeable: it was
previously only recorded on the first send, which meant the first repo you
ever picked was the one you were stuck with.

patch.summary: Record the pull request a piece of feedback turned into.

post.summary:
`POST /api/v1/invites/accept` — the signed-in person consumes a token. On a
match (pending, unexpired, email equals the signed-in email) they are added
to the tenant with the invite's role — linked by `person_id`, keeping their
personal tenant — and the invite becomes `accepted`. Any failure leaves the
invite untouched and returns the caller to their own tenant with a message
(AC-4/5); already-a-member is a no-op success (AC-6).
Create, or return what is already there.
post.description:
`200` on an existing name rather than `409`: callers want the label to
exist, not to be told who created it first.

post.summary: POST /api/v1/me/tenant — switch the browser session's active tenant.
post.description:
Membership is enforced here (403 for a tenant the person does not belong
to), and the switch is a single UPDATE of `sessions_auth`: because
`AuthCtx` resolves BOTH `user_id` and `tenant_id` from that row on every
request, moving the row re-scopes every tenant-scoped surface at once. The
per-tenant `user_id` changes too, so attribution and role follow the tenant.

Browser sessions only (NG-5): a `nook_user_` token has no `sessions_auth`
row, so the UPDATE affects nothing and the endpoint says so rather than
pretending to switch a credential that stays bound to its tenant.

get.summary:
GET /api/v1/me/tenants — every tenant the signed-in person belongs to, with
the active one marked. The switcher reads it; `me` also carries it inline so
the first render needs no second request.
What this control plane can hand out, and how to ask for it.

post.summary: Trade a join token for a certificate signed by the tenant's CA.

POST /api/v1/nodes/join — unauthenticated; the join token IS the
credential. Idempotent per (tenant, name): re-joining an existing node
rotates its token instead of creating a duplicate, which keeps container
reboots predictable. Join tokens stay valid until expiry (M1 simplicity);
`used_at` records last use for audit.
POST /api/v1/nodes/join-tokens — mint a token to enroll a new machine.
Renew on the strength of the key the node already holds.
post.description:
Works whether the certificate expired five minutes or five months ago, and
whether or not the tenant's CA rotated meanwhile — which is precisely why
the response carries the whole trust bundle rather than just a certificate.

post.summary:
Ask a node to rescan its workspace roots now, instead of waiting for the
periodic sweep. Backs `nook import`.
Cut a machine off.
post.description:
Distinct from expiry on purpose: a certificate simply running out means
"renew me", while revocation means "never again". Collapsing the two would
let a compromised machine wait out its certificate and quietly come back.

post.summary:
Open an ad-hoc terminal on a machine — a shell in the node's home directory,
no workspace required. The "just give me a prompt on that box" path.
POST /api/v1/nodes/{id}/update — tell a node to replace its agent and restart.
post.description:
A person asking, rather than the automatic path: a node already updates
itself on reconnect when its version differs from what this control plane
expects. This is for the case where you do not want to wait for a
reconnect, or where the automatic path declined and you want to see why.

The node decides whether it can. It knows whether anything would restart it
and refuses if not, because an agent that replaces its binary and exits
unsupervised simply goes offline — and doing that across a fleet takes every
machine at once.

get.summary:
What providers exist and what each one needs, so the UI builds its forms
from the server rather than from a copy of this list that drifts.

post.summary: Send a sample through one channel and report what happened.

parameters.name: unread
parameters.description: Only what has not been read.

post.summary: Mark one read, or all of them when no id is given.
Raise a notification.
post.description:
Deliberately open to any authenticated caller INCLUDING a node token: the
whole point is that a machine finishing a job can say so. A node can already
report events about itself; this is the same trust, with a nicer surface.

get.summary: The audit trail, including operator reads themselves.

Who holds what. Needed before granting is meaningful — you cannot revoke a
binding you cannot see.

post.summary: Grant or revoke a role binding.
post.description:
The one write on this surface, and it is here rather than deferred with the
others because a deployment with exactly one operator and no way to make a
second is a deployment one lost password away from being unadministrable.

Requires `org.manage` — an operator can appoint another operator, which is
the same authority every root-shaped role has. A tenant admin cannot,
because `org.manage` is not in their role.

get.summary: Nodes, always visible. Names, status, resources, owner, session count.
The current policy for one org, for the operator who may change it.

post.summary: Widen or narrow one field. Recorded, and announced to the people it affects.

get.summary: Tenants, at minimum visibility.
get.description:
Always visible, per the model: that a tenant exists, its member count, and
how many nodes and sessions it runs. Several machines working one task is an
audit signal, and an operator who cannot see load cannot run the deployment.

Never visible here: repository names, branches, worktree paths, task titles.
Those are policy-gated and added by `enrich` below — they are not selected
and then removed.

post.summary: Stage a new CA for a tenant.
post.description:
Two steps, not one. Staging distributes the new authority so machines pick
it up on their next renewal; promoting makes it sign. A single "rotate"
button that did both would strand every node that had not renewed in
between — which is the reason the tenant-facing route has always been two
calls, and not a limitation worth papering over here.

Delegates to the same mechanism rather than reimplementing it: there must be
exactly one way a CA is created, or the two drift and one is wrong.

post.summary: Promote a staged CA to signer. The previous signer keeps being trusted.
Move a tenant into another org.
post.description:
Requires `org.manage` at BOTH ends — the org losing it and the org gaining
it. Checking only one would let somebody with authority over a single org
pull tenants into it from orgs they have no say over, or push their own
tenants somewhere they cannot be followed.

get.summary: Resolve the "Auto (best available)" node the UI leaves selected by default.

parameters.name: workspace_id
parameters.description: Prefer a node that already hosts this workspace.

get.summary:
`GET /api/v1/sessions/agent-states` — every live agent state in the tenant,
so a browser that just loaded shows the right spinners immediately rather
than a blank tab until the next transition. Stale entries are swept here.

delete.summary:
Remove a session record. Kills the tmux session first when it's still
alive, so "delete" never leaves an orphan running on a node.

post.summary:
`POST /api/v1/sessions/{id}/agent-state` — a hook says what its agent is
doing. Guarded by session-content access (only someone who could see the
terminal may report on it), it stores the state and, on a real change,
fans a `SessionAgentState` event to every browser in the tenant. The report
is ephemeral — nothing is written to the database and no notification is
raised — so a per-turn `running`/`idle` stream costs the inbox nothing.
Type into a session, as if a human were at the keyboard.
post.description:
This is what makes a session drivable from a script: no browser, no SSH,
no tmux knowledge — the runtime on the other end (claude, hermes, bash)
sees ordinary keystrokes. `enter` is on by default because a prompt left
sitting unsubmitted is never what the caller meant.

post.summary:
Read back what a session is showing — the visible screen plus optional
scrollback. The other half of driving a session from a script: send, then
look at what happened.

Bring a dead session back: same record, same tabs, fresh tmux session.
A terminal you closed (or a runtime that exited) shouldn't strand the
session — the node's `start` is idempotent, so this just re-issues it.

The terminals inside a session — tmux windows. Listing, opening, splitting,
focusing, closing and renaming all go through here and always answer with
the resulting list.

Teach the fleet. Re-teaching the same name replaces it, because "I improved
the skill, push it everywhere" is the common case and it has to be one verb.

delete.summary: Unteach: forget it here, and tell every node to remove it.

get.summary: The pick query.
get.description:
Built as one statement with bound parameters rather than assembled from
strings — every filter here is caller-supplied, and a query builder that
interpolated any of them would be an injection with a board's worth of data
behind it. The cost is a slightly denser SQL body; the benefit is that no
value ever reaches the parser.

parameters.name: board
parameters.description: Board id or key (`ENG`). Omit to search the whole tenant.

parameters.name: assignee
parameters.description: A user id, or the literal `none` for unassigned.

parameters.name: is_blocked
parameters.description: Filter on the derived blocker state.

parameters.name: archived
parameters.description:
Include archived tasks. Default (absent/false) excludes them, so the
agent pick can never claim archived work (MAIN-15 AC-2).

parameters.name: cursor
parameters.description: Opaque: the `created_at` of the last row of the previous page.

post.summary: Take the work, atomically.
post.description:
The assignment and the move are one statement with the "still unassigned"
test in its WHERE clause, so two agents racing cannot both win: the second
UPDATE matches zero rows and gets a 409 carrying the current state, which is
enough for it to pick again without another round trip.

put.summary: Attach a label. Succeeds when it is already attached.

delete.summary: Detach a label. Succeeds when it is already absent.

post.summary: Give the work back: clear the assignee so somebody else can pick it up.

get.summary: What this tenant trusts, what signs, and how the rotation is progressing.

post.summary: Stage a new CA: trusted immediately, signing nothing yet.
post.description:
Step one of a rotation — distribute before switching, so machines learn the
new CA on their next renewal and nothing breaks when it starts signing.

delete.summary: Drop a CA from the trust bundle. Refused while it still has live leaves.

post.summary:
Make a staged CA the signer. The previous signer becomes `retiring` —
still trusted, no longer issuing.

get.summary: Every tenant this user is a member of, with the role they hold in each.

`GET /api/v1/tenants/{id}/invites` — pending invites (never the token).
owner/admin only.

post.summary:
`POST /api/v1/tenants/{id}/invites` — create a pending invite, returning the
accept URL. Re-inviting the same email replaces the existing pending invite
(AC-2). owner/admin only.

delete.summary:
`DELETE /api/v1/tenants/{id}/invites/{invite}` — revoke a pending invite; its
link stops working. owner/admin only.

post.summary:
`POST /api/v1/tenants/{id}/invites/{invite}/resend` — re-email the pending
invite's accept link (AC-5). owner/admin only.

`POST /api/v1/tenants/{id}/leave` — remove your own membership. You keep your
personal tenant; the last owner cannot leave (that includes your personal
tenant, where you are the sole owner) (AC-4, AC-5).

get.summary:
`GET /api/v1/tenants/{id}/members` — everyone in the tenant. Any member may
view (AC-1); management is gated separately.

delete.summary:
`DELETE /api/v1/tenants/{id}/members/{pid}` — remove a member. owner/admin
only; the last owner cannot be removed (AC-3, AC-5). The `users` row and all
authored work stay with the tenant (NG-2); only the membership grant is gone,
so the person loses access on their next request.

patch.summary:
`PATCH /api/v1/tenants/{id}/members/{pid}` — change a member's role.
owner/admin may move member↔admin; only an owner may grant `owner`
(co-owner / transfer). The last owner cannot be demoted (AC-2, AC-5).

get.summary:
List this user's tokens. Never the tokens themselves — the point of the
list is deciding which one to revoke.

post.summary: Mint a token for the signed-in user.
post.description:
Requires a *user* — a node token minting user tokens would be a machine
promoting itself to its owner, which is the one thing node confinement
exists to prevent.

delete.summary: Revoke one. Immediate: the next request carrying it is unauthorized.

get.summary:
Passkeys enrolled on this vault, with their wrapped secrets — the browser
needs the blob in hand before it can ask the authenticator to open it.

post.summary:
Enrol a passkey. Requires the app password, since that's what's being
wrapped — you can't hand out a key to a vault you can't open.

delete.summary: Forget a passkey. The vault is untouched — the app password still opens it.

post.summary: Note that a passkey was just used, so the settings page can show it.

Set the app password. Once only — a second attempt is a conflict, not an
overwrite, so a stray call can never orphan existing secrets.

get.summary: Has this user set an app password yet?

post.summary:
Check a password without decrypting anything — lets the UI unlock (and
hold the password for syncing) with a clear yes/no.

409.description: the workspace still has live sessions

patch.summary: Rename a workspace — the label only.
patch.description:
Deliberately does NOT touch the slug, the checkouts on disk, or the git
remote: those are the workspace's identity, and rediscovery matches on
them. So calling a clone of `acme/services` "the flaky one" costs nothing
and breaks nothing — no directory moves, no session loses its path, and
the next heartbeat won't create a duplicate workspace.

get.summary: Live git status + working-tree diff, relayed from the node.

post.summary: Stage everything and commit, on the machine that holds the checkout.
post.description:
Finishing work shouldn't require dropping into a terminal to type the two
commands you were always going to type — you just read the diff in the panel
above the button.

post.summary: Push the checkout's current branch.

Adopt a checkout's existing `.env` into the vault, sealed with the app
password.
post.description:
Importing a repo that already carries a `.env` is the common case, and
leaving that file outside the vault meant it never travelled to the user's
other machines — and never got encrypted. This is the on-ramp: read it off
disk once, seal it, and from then on it syncs like any other secret.

get.summary:
Does this workspace have a `.env` sitting in a checkout that the vault
doesn't know about? Asked right after an import, so we only interrupt
someone for their password when there's actually something to seal.

post.summary:
Unlock a passphrase-sealed secret. The passphrase is never stored; a wrong
one is reported as such rather than as a decryption error.

get.summary:
Readiness: 200 only when the database is reachable. Kubernetes pulls a pod
that fails this from Service endpoints but does NOT restart it — a DB blip
should stop routing traffic, not recycle the process.

Liveness: 200 whenever the process is up, deliberately WITHOUT touching the
database. Used as a Kubernetes liveness probe, this must not depend on any
external dependency — otherwise a brief DB outage would make the cluster
kill and restart otherwise-healthy pods, turning a hiccup into an outage.
Takes no `State` at all, so it cannot grow a database call by accident.

AcceptInviteResult.description:
The outcome of accepting (or failing to accept) an invite. Whichever tenant
the person ends up in, the UI switches/refetches to it.

accepted.description: True when the person is now a member of the invited tenant.

tenant_id.description: The tenant to land in — the shared one on success, else the person's own.

AddPasskeyRequest.description:
Enrolling a passkey. The wrapping happens in the browser; the server only
ever sees the sealed blob.

AgentStateItem.description: One session's current agent state, for seeding the UI on load.

AttachClientMessage.description: Terminal attach socket messages (browser → control plane).

oneOf.description:
The agreed terminal grid: the PTY is sized to the LARGEST current
viewer; every viewer renders this grid (scaling its font down if its
panel is smaller), so a small window never shrinks the session for
everyone else.

data.description:
The agreed terminal grid: the PTY is sized to the LARGEST current
viewer; every viewer renders this grid (scaling its font down if its
panel is smaller), so a small window never shrinks the session for
everyone else.

AttachServerMessage.description: Terminal attach socket messages (control plane → browser).

dev_login.description: The dev/CI escape hatch is enabled (never in production).

device_authorization_endpoint.description:
Where a native client starts a device authorization.

Read from the IdP's discovery document. `None` means the provider does
not advertise one — in which case no compliant client can start the
flow, whatever else the provider supports.

device_client_id.description:
Public client id for native clients. Distinct from the control plane's
own client, which is confidential and must not ship inside an app.

local.description: Username and password held in this database.

oidc.description: An OIDC identity provider is configured.

oidc_issuer.description:
The identity provider itself, for clients that must talk to it
directly.

A desktop app cannot receive the browser redirect this control plane
registered, so it uses the device authorization grant against the IdP.
It learns where to go from here rather than from its own configuration:
the operator sets the IdP up once, on the server, and every client
follows.

BindingRow.description: Who holds what, for the roles table.

scope_label.description: The org or tenant slug the binding is scoped to, when it has one.

Board.description:
Provider values: `local` | `jira` | `github` | `linear` | `trello`.
External boards remain authoritative; NookOS federates.

key.description:
The prefix in `NOOK-42`. Unique per tenant, derived from the name when
not given, and immutable once assigned — it is written into PR bodies
and branch names, which no rename can reach back and fix.

type.description:
What this column MEANS, independent of what it is called:
`backlog` | `unstarted` | `started` | `completed` | `canceled`.

Automation targets the type so that renaming "In Progress" to "Doing"
is a cosmetic change rather than a broken loop. The name is for people.

Capabilities.description:
What a node reports about itself on registration. The control plane never
inspects a machine — the node describes its own capabilities.

agent_version.description:
The agent's own version. Reported like everything else here, so
"which machines are behind?" needs no column of its own — this whole
struct is already stored as jsonb on the node.

ssh_public_key.description:
This node's SSH public key (generated locally; the private half never
leaves the machine). Add it as a deploy key to clone private repos.

Capability.description: What the signed-in caller may do, so a UI can hide what it cannot offer.

deployment.description: Permission keys held at the deployment scope.

operator.description:
Holds an operator binding somewhere — drives whether the operator
section appears at all.

org_id.description: The org this caller's tenant belongs to, for reading its policy.

ChangeMemberRoleRequest.description:
Change a member's role. `member` ↔ `admin` for any owner/admin; `owner`
(a co-owner / transfer) is owner-only.

secret.description: Masked in the UI and never read back.

ChannelKind.description: What a channel kind needs, so the UI can build a form without hardcoding it.

ClaimTaskRequest.description: `POST /tasks/{id}/claim` — take the work without racing another agent.

oneOf.description:
Claim on behalf of this user rather than the caller. For a human
assigning work; agents omit it.

column_type.description:
Move the task here at the same time, by column TYPE. Omit to claim
without moving.

background.description:
Return as soon as the node has been asked, instead of waiting for the
clone to finish. Progress arrives as activity events carrying `job_id`.

oneOf.description: Tenant git credential to clone with (private repos over SSH).

name.description: Directory name; derived from the URL when omitted.

key.description: Omit to derive one from the name.

config.description: Provider-specific. Write-only: it is never read back.

author_name.description:
How an agent signs its work, e.g. `"loop-build on azul"`.

NookOS has no separate agent identity — an agent acts under a person's
token, so the honest record is "this user's credential, used by this
tool". Supplying a name says which tool; it does not grant anything,
and the underlying `author_id` remains the real user.

generate.description: …or let the server generate an ed25519 keypair.

private_key.description: Paste an existing private key (OpenSSH PEM)…

agent_url.description:
Where the joining machine should point its **agent** connection.

Not always the API's address. The agent listener terminates TLS in the
control-plane process — only it can judge a client certificate against
the right tenant's CA — so it cannot sit behind the proxy that fronts
the API, and deployments routinely give it its own name. A node told
only the API address would enrol against a URL it must not use.

ca_fingerprint.description:
SHA-256 of the certificate the joining machine should expect to see,
so it can pin the server before handing over anything. `None` when the
control plane does not terminate TLS itself (dev, or TLS at the edge),
in which case there is nothing honest to pin to.

token.description: Shown exactly once; only a hash is stored.

path.description:
Pin the session to a specific checkout path (e.g. a worktree). When
omitted, the workspace's first checkout on the node is used.

column_type.description:
Place by semantic state instead of by id — what automation wants, since
it knows "the backlog" but not which uuid that is today.

labels.description:
Label NAMES, created for the tenant if new. Names rather than ids
because a filer knows `agent-ready`, not its uuid.

CreateTerminalRequest.description:
Open an ad-hoc terminal on a machine — a shell with no workspace, running in
the node's home directory. What you reach for when you just want a prompt on
a box, not to start work on a project.

name.description: Name the session; defaults to something like "bash · <node>".

runtime.description: The runtime to run — `bash` by default, but any the node has installed.

CreateUserTokenRequest.description: Asking for a personal access token.

expires_in_days.description: Expire it after this many days. Omit for a token that doesn't expire.

name.description: What it's for ("laptop cli", "ci"). Shown in the list you revoke from.

CreateUserTokenResponse.description: The one and only time the token itself is readable.

token.description: `nook_user_…` — store it now; the server keeps only its hash.

DeleteWorkspaceRequest.description:
Deleting a workspace. Records always go; the checkouts on disk only go
when explicitly asked for (and if they stay, discovery re-adds them).

delete_files.description: Also delete the checkout directories on every online node.

DeleteWorkspaceResponse.description: What a workspace delete actually did.

checkouts_remaining.description:
Checkouts left behind (node offline, or removal failed) — these will
be rediscovered.

DevAccount.description:
One account you can sign in as, in dev mode only.

Exists so a person can switch between users without inventing credentials —
testing "what does an operator see that a member does not" is impossible if
becoming the other person is hard.

deployment_roles.description:
Role keys held at the deployment scope — so the picker can show which
of these accounts is the operator without you having to remember.

DiscoveredWorkspace.description:
A git repository found under a node's workspace roots. Repositories are
self-describing; the node reports, the control plane reconciles.

worktree.description:
True when this checkout is a linked git worktree (its `.git` is a file
pointing at the primary repo, not a directory).

DispatchSuggestion.description:
The dispatcher recommends; humans approve. It never codes, edits, or
deploys — suggestions are the entire output surface.

EmailVerificationStatus.description:
Whether the signed-in user's email is verified, and whether a local
verification round-trip applies to them (MAIN-30).

can_request.description:
True for a local account that can request a verification email. OIDC
users are verified upstream and cannot request one here (NG-1).

EnrollRequest.description:
First contact: trade a join token for a certificate.

The node generates its keypair locally and sends only a CSR — the private
key never leaves the machine, so the control plane cannot leak what it was
never given.

name.description: Name for a machine enrolling for the first time.

token.description: `nook_join_…`, which is what decides whose CA signs this.

EnrollResponse.description: A certificate plus the trust the node needs to verify its peer.

ca_bundle.description:
EVERY CA this tenant trusts, not just the signer. A node that refreshed
only its own certificate would stay pinned to a CA being retired, which
is what turns a rotation into an outage.

Event.description:
Everything produces events. Kind is an open dotted string:
"node.connected", "session.started", "task.moved", "user.login", ...

next_cursor.description: Pass as `before` to fetch the next (older) page.

FeedbackItem.description: One improvement someone asked for, and what became of it.

FeedbackTarget.description: Where feedback goes — the first-run question this answers is "which repo?"

branch.description:
Branch the agent is told to work on, so improvements land somewhere
isolated and deployable rather than on whatever was checked out.

instructions.description:
What the agent should do with the change once it works — reviewed,
pushed, PR'd, left uncommitted. Overrides the built-in wording.

instructions_from_repo.description:
True when the instructions came from `.nook-feedback.md` in the repo
rather than from this setting, so the UI can say where they live.

GitCommitRequest.description: Commit everything in a checkout, from the git panel.

node_id.description: Which machine's checkout — a workspace can exist on several.

GitCredential.description: A tenant git credential — only the public half is ever returned.

oneOf.description: Tenant git credential to push with. Omit to use the node's own key.

diff.description: Unified diff of the working tree (truncated by the node if huge).

is_repo.description:
`false` when the checkout is not a git repository — a "+ New empty
project" directory, say. Everything below is then empty for a reason
that is not "nothing has changed", and the UI hides the panel instead
of reporting a clean tree.

ImportSecretRequest.description: Adopt a file that already exists in a checkout into the vault.

passphrase.description:
The app password. Same rule as saving: nothing enters the vault
unsealed.

Invite.description:
A pending/accepted/revoked invitation into a tenant. `accept_url` is set only
on the create response (the link to hand out); the token is never listed.

accept_url.description: The accept link, returned only when the invite is created (never listed).

JoinRequest.description: Sent by `nook join` (unauthenticated; the join token IS the credential).

node_token.description: Long-lived node credential; shown once, stored hashed.

Label.description:
A tenant-wide label. `agent-ready` is the human approval gate: the one
signal that says an agent may pick this up, and deliberately not something
an agent can apply to itself.

available.description: Local sign-in is possible: the tenant is undecided, or already local.

mode.description: "oidc" | "local" | null when nobody has signed in yet.

needs_bootstrap.description: No account exists yet, so the first visitor can claim this instance.

MeResponse.description: The signed-in caller with their tenant.

capability.description:
What this caller may do, so a UI can hide what it cannot offer rather
than rendering a button that 403s.

tenants.description:
Every tenant this person belongs to (from `tenant_members`), with the
active one marked `current`. Carried on `me` so the UI can render a
tenant switcher without a second request. A person in exactly one tenant
gets a one-element list, and the UI shows a plain label for that case.

resources.description: Latest heartbeat resource sample (see `NodeResources`); `{}` until first.

NodeArtifact.description:
A node binary this control plane can hand out. One per platform it was
built with — the fleet stays on one version because the server only offers
the build it shipped with.

label.description: Human label for the picker ("macOS · Apple silicon").

url.description:
Where to download it — a GitHub release asset. The control plane no
longer hosts binaries, so it deliberately reports neither size nor
checksum: it cannot attest to bytes it does not serve, and a stale
digest is worse than none.

NodeReleases.description:
Everything the "add node" flow needs: what to download, and where the
one-shot installer lives.

base_url.description: This instance as the caller reached it — what a new machine should use.

install_url.description: URL of the generated install script.

version.description: Version of the control plane, which is the version of these binaries.

NodeResources.description:
Live resource sample a node reports on each heartbeat, so both humans and
the triage scheduler can see which machine can take the workload.

active_sessions.description: NookOS-managed sessions currently alive on the node.

load_avg1.description: 1-minute load average (0 on platforms without it).

NodeWorkspace.description:
A workspace checked out at a path on a particular node — the join table
that lets one workspace exist on many machines.

Notification.description:
Something a person should see. Distinct from an `Event`, which is the
complete record of what happened and is never marked read.

kind.description: The dotted event kind that produced it, or `custom`.

NotificationChannel.description:
A configured delivery channel.

`config` is deliberately absent: it holds bot tokens and webhook URLs, and
a channel list is the sort of thing a UI fetches often and logs freely.
What a person needs to see is that it exists, whether it works, and what it
is filtered to.

NotifyRequest.description:
Raise a notification by hand — what `nook notify` and an agent's finish
hook both call.

kind.description: Defaults to `custom`. Channels filter on this.

session.description:
The session this notification is about, when it comes from an agent hook.
The control plane turns it into a deep link to the terminal (using its
own public URL, which the node does not know), so clicking "an agent is
waiting on you" opens the session — and external channels get a real URL,
not a path. An explicit `link` still wins.

OpResponse.description: Outcome of a long-running git operation on a node.

OperatorAuditEntry.description:
An audit row. Kinds, actors and times — never payloads, which can carry the
very metadata policy exists to gate.

OperatorTenant.description:
A tenant as an operator sees it.

The first block is always visible: existence, counts, load. Everything
after is `Option` and stays `None` unless the org opted in — policy ADDS
these fields rather than filtering them out, so forgetting to add one
leaves it absent instead of leaking it.

Org.description: An org: the layer between a deployment and its tenants.

PolicyField.description: One policy-gated field with its current state and plain-language meaning.

description.description: Written for a person, not a developer — every user is shown this.

ephemeral.description: Wipe the synced file from checkouts when the session ends.

passphrase.description:
The app password. Required, not optional: a `.env` moves between
machines, so it is sealed with something the server never stores
before the app key wraps it. A database dump plus `SECRETS_KEY` must
never be enough to read one.

RelatedTask.description:
The other end of a relation, with enough to render it without a second
fetch.

column_type.description: The column type of the other task — what makes a blocker resolved.

RenameWorkspaceRequest.description:
A new label for a workspace. The name is what people read; the slug, the
checkouts on disk and the git remote are its identity, and none of them
move — which is what makes this safe to do on a whim.

RenewRequest.description:
Renewal: a node asks for a fresh certificate using the key it already has.

Deliberately no join token. Tokens are for a machine with no key yet;
requiring one at renewal would mean expiry costs a manual re-join, which is
exactly what must never happen to a laptop that was closed for a month.

ReportAgentStateRequest.description: A hook reporting what the agent in a session is doing.

window.description: The tmux window the agent runs in, so the right terminal chip lights up.

RequestVerificationResult.description:
The outcome of requesting a verification email — best-effort send, so a
mail-transport failure is reported here rather than failing the request.

ScheduledNode.description: The node the resource-aware scheduler chose for "Auto" placement.

SecretOnDisk.description: Whether an import left a `.env` on disk that the vault hasn't adopted yet.

checkout_path.description: Which checkout it was found in.

in_vault.description: Already stored in the vault, so there's nothing to adopt.

Session.description:
Status values: `starting` | `running` | `detached` | `exited` | `error`.
Runtime is an open string: "claude", "hermes", "codex", "bash", "zsh", ...

error.description: Why the session failed to start, when it did.

oneOf.description:
The workspace this session runs in, or `None` for an ad-hoc terminal —
a plain shell opened on a machine with no project behind it, running in
the node's home directory.

SessionInputRequest.description:
Keystrokes for a session. What a script sends instead of attaching a
terminal.

enter.description:
Press Enter afterwards. Defaults to true: an unsubmitted prompt is
almost never what a caller wanted.

SessionOutputRequest.description: How much of a session's screen to read back.

history_lines.description: Scrollback lines above the visible screen (0–2000). Default 0.

SessionOutputResponse.description:
A session's current screen, with enough context to know what you're
looking at — `runtime` is how a caller tells a claude shell from a bash one.

SessionWindow.description: One terminal inside a session (a tmux window).

panes.description: Panes in this window — >1 means it's split.

SetFeedbackTargetRequest.description:
Point feedback at a repo and a branch. Separate from submitting, so the
target can be changed at any time rather than only on the first send.

branch.description: Empty means "leave the agent to pick"; a name pins every change to it.

instructions.description:
Empty falls back to `.nook-feedback.md` in the repo, then to the
built-in wording.

SetVaultPassphraseRequest.description: Setting or checking the app password.

Skill.description:
A skill taught to the whole fleet.

Stored by the control plane rather than pushed and forgotten, so that a node
which was offline when it was taught — or which joins next week — converges
on register instead of quietly being the one machine that never learned it.

name.description: Becomes a path component on every machine: `<skills>/<name>/SKILL.md`.

sha256.description:
Of `content`. Lets a node skip a write it already has, and lets an
operator see whether two machines really do hold the same thing.

SkillSummary.description:
The same thing without its body — a list of twenty skills should not ship
twenty documents to draw a table.

size.description: Bytes, so the UI can show a size without holding the content.

runtime.description: Runtime for the feedback session (defaults to claude).

oneOf.description:
Where this feedback should be worked on. Remembered for next time;
required only until one has been chosen.

SwitchTenantRequest.description:
Switch the browser session's active tenant. The caller must be a member of
the target tenant, or the endpoint returns 403.

TaskComment.description:
Durable discussion on a task: the builder's blocking question, the
reviewer's verdict, the human's answer.

author_name.description:
Denormalised, so an agent with no users row — and a deleted user —
still render with attribution.

TaskDetail.description: One whole issue: what the loop reads before it starts work.

blocked_by.description: Tasks that must finish before this one can start.

is_blocked.description:
Derived from the blockers' column types, never stored — a stored flag
would drift the moment a blocker moved.

archived_at.description:
When this task was archived off the board, or `None` while it is live.
Archived tasks are hidden from the board by default and excluded from the
agent pick query, but the row is preserved and unarchiving clears this.

oneOf.description: Node the triage scheduler chose (or you forced) to run this work.

key.description:
`NOOK-42` — the board's key and this task's number. Computed, not
stored: storing it would let it disagree with the two columns it is
made of.

labels.description:
Every label on this task. Populated by one query for a whole board
rather than one per task.

number.description:
Per-board sequence behind the human key. `None` only for a task created
before keys existed and not yet backfilled.

priority.description:
`0` none, `1` urgent, `2` high, `3` medium, `4` low — Linear's
convention, so values port cleanly. Note `0` sorts LAST: "nobody set a
priority" is not a claim that the work is least important.

url.description:
Absolute deep link into the web UI, so an agent reporting "filed
NOOK-42" can give a human something to click.

name.description:
Omitted means "derive it": from the document's own frontmatter `name:`,
falling back to the filename. Explicit wins, because a file called
SKILL.md says nothing about what it teaches.

delivered_to.description: Nodes the fan-out actually reached. The rest converge on reconnect.

offline.description:
Nodes known to this tenant that were offline, named rather than
counted — "3 nodes were offline" is not something an operator can act
on, and silence about them would be worse.

TenantCaSummary.description:
A tenant CA as an admin sees it. Never the private key — it is not
exportable, by design.

nodes_holding_leaves.description:
Machines still holding an unexpired leaf from this CA — the number that
says whether it can be retired yet.

TenantMemberItem.description:
One member OF a tenant, for the members panel — distinct from
`TenantMembership` (a tenant the caller belongs to). Keyed by
`principal_id`, the `users.id`/`tenant_members.principal_id` used to change
the role or remove them.

TenantMembership.description:
A tenant the caller belongs to, and the role they hold in it.

Membership is deliberately its own concept: a user has one *current*
tenant (`users.tenant_id`) and may reach several, which is what teams will
be — not a new mechanism, just more rows.

current.description: The tenant this session is scoped to right now.

oneOf.description: NULL = built-in theme shipped with NookOS.

ThemeTokens.description:
Design tokens applied as CSS custom properties. Every visual aspect is
configurable; unknown keys pass through untouched so theme packs can extend.

oneOf.description:
What the agent in a session is doing right now: `running`, `waiting`
(blocked on a human), or `idle`. Driven by Claude Code hooks reporting
through `nook agent-state`, so the terminal tabs can show a spinner vs a
"needs you" mark without anyone watching the output. `window` is the
tmux window index the agent runs in, so the right in-session terminal
chip lights up and the shells beside it do not.

data.description:
What the agent in a session is doing right now: `running`, `waiting`
(blocked on a human), or `idle`. Driven by Claude Code hooks reporting
through `nook agent-state`, so the terminal tabs can show a spinner vs a
"needs you" mark without anyone watching the output. `window` is the
tmux window index the agent runs in, so the right in-session terminal
chip lights up and the shells beside it do not.

oneOf.description:
Something a person should see, right now.

Carries the whole notification rather than an id, unlike `TaskChanged`.
The distinction is what the client does with it: a task id says "refetch
that", and the client already knows how. A notification has no canonical
place to be refetched from — it IS the message — and a toast that had to
round-trip before it could be shown would arrive after the moment it was
about.

data.description:
Something a person should see, right now.

Carries the whole notification rather than an id, unlike `TaskChanged`.
The distinction is what the client does with it: a task id says "refetch
that", and the client already knows how. A notification has no canonical
place to be refetched from — it IS the message — and a toast that had to
round-trip before it could be shown would arrive after the moment it was
about.

oneOf.description:
A task changed — moved, relabelled, commented on, claimed.

Carries only the id, not the task. Agents and other browsers change
tasks constantly, and a payload would be a second copy of state that
arrives out of order with the fetch the viewer is already doing. The id
says "what you have for this one is stale"; the client refetches what it
actually needs, which for a board is one card and for an open detail
panel is the whole issue.

data.description:
A task changed — moved, relabelled, commented on, claimed.

Carries only the id, not the task. Agents and other browsers change
tasks constantly, and a payload would be a second copy of state that
arrives out of order with the fetch the viewer is already doing. The id
says "what you have for this one is stale"; the client refetches what it
actually needs, which for a board is one card and for an open detail
panel is the whole issue.

UiEvent.description: Live events pushed to browsers over `/api/v1/ws/ui`.

key.description:
Change the prefix in `NOOK-42`.

Normally immutable — it is written into PR bodies and branch names that
no rename can reach back and fix — but settable, because a key derived
from a board name is sometimes just wrong ("NookOS Bootstrap" derives
"NOOKO") and living with it forever is worse than an explicit change a
person chose.

config.description: Omit to keep the stored secrets untouched.

UpdateSessionRequest.description: Renaming a session (tabs are named things people recognize).

expected_updated_at.description:
Optimistic-concurrency precondition. When set, the update applies only
if the task's current `updated_at` still equals this; a mismatch makes
NO change and returns `409 Conflict` (the body changed under the caller).
The body-editing surfaces send it; a move / other unguarded PATCH leaves
it absent and behaves exactly as before (MAIN-36).

workspace_id.description:
Which workspace this task belongs to. Absent leaves it alone, `null`
clears it, an id sets it.

Nested because those are three cases, not two, and every other field
here only has two. A confined `/loop-build` agent claims only tasks in
its own workspace, so an unscoped task is one no loop will ever pick
up — and until this field existed there was no way to scope one after
filing it. Clearing has to be expressible too, or a wrong answer is
permanent.

User.description: Role values: `owner` | `admin` | `member` (TEXT CHECK in the schema).

UserToken.description: A personal access token as listed back — everything except the secret.

VaultPasskey.description: A passkey enrolled to unlock the vault.

credential_id.description:
Base64url WebAuthn credential id, so the browser can ask for this
specific passkey.

wrapped_secret.description:
The app password sealed under the passkey-derived key, base64. Only
the browser that holds the passkey can open it.

VaultStatus.description: Whether this user has an app password (the key that seals their secrets).

passkeys.description:
How many passkeys can unlock this vault. Non-zero means the UI should
reach for a passkey before asking anyone to type a password.

oneOf.description: Open another terminal in the session and focus it.
Split the visible terminal so two are on screen at once.

WindowAction.description: What to do with a session's terminals (tmux windows/panes).

WorkspaceLocation.description: A workspace location as presented to the UI (node join included).

worktree.description: This checkout is a linked git worktree of the workspace's primary repo.

WorkspaceSecret.description: A workspace secret file (e.g. .env). Content only present on single-get.

ephemeral.description: Removed from checkouts when the session ends.

protected.description: Sealed with a passphrase — reading it needs that passphrase.
